title: Kangla Fort
tagline: Kangla Fort — heritage city of Manipur

overview.short: The Kangla (with diacritic, Kanglā), officially known as the Kangla Fort, is an old fortified palace at Imphal, in the Manipur state of India.
overview.description: The Kangla (with diacritic, Kanglā), officially known as the Kangla Fort, is an old fortified palace at Imphal, in the Manipur state of India. It was formerly situated on both sides (western and eastern) of the bank of the Imphal River, now remaining only on the western side in ruined conditions. Kangla means "the prominent part of the dry land" in old Meetei. It was the traditional seat of the past Meetei rulers of Manipur.

howToReach.roadNote: Roads are generally good; check seasonal/monsoon conditions before high-altitude or remote stretches.

topPlaces.name: Statue of Meidingu Nara Singh
topPlaces.description: The Statue of Meidingu Nara Singh, also known as the Statue of Maharaja Narasingh (Meitei: Meidingu Narasinghgi Mitam), is a bronze sculpture located at the Kangla Sanathong, the western entrance gate to the Kangla Fort in Imphal. Meidingu Nara Singh (1844-1850 A.D.) was a Meitei monarch and the sovereign of Kangleipak (Meitei for 'Manipur kingdom').

topPlaces.name: Kangla Sanathong
topPlaces.description: The Kangla Sanathong (Meitei for 'The Royal Entrance to the Kangla'), also known as the Kangla Gate, is the western entrance gate to the Kangla Fort in Imphal West district of Kangleipak (Meitei for 'Manipur').

topPlaces.name: Imphal
topPlaces.description: Imphal (UK: IM-fahl, US: IMP-hul; Meitei: [impʰal]) is the capital city of the Indian state of Manipur. The metropolitan centre of the city contains the ruins of Kangla Palace (officially known as Kangla Fort), the royal seat of the former Kingdom of Manipur, surrounded by a moat.

topPlaces.name: Pakhangba Temple, Kangla
topPlaces.description: The Pakhangba Temple, also known as the Pakhangba Laishang (Meitei: ꯄꯥꯈꯪꯕ ꯂꯥꯏꯁꯪ, lit. 'Temple of God Pakhangba'), is a Meitei temple dedicated to the God Pakhangba of Sanamahism, the traditional Meitei religion, located beside the Nungseng Eekon, to the left side of the Kangla Sanathong, the western gate of the Kangla Fort in the Imphal West district of Manipur.

topPlaces.name: Manipur
topPlaces.description: Manipur (, IPA: [məɳiˈpʊɾ]) is a state in northeastern India, with Imphal as its capital city. It borders the Indian states of Assam to the west, Mizoram to the south, and Nagaland to the north and shares the international border with Myanmar, specifically the Sagaing Region to the east and Chin State to the southeast.

topPlaces.name: Manung Kangjeibung
topPlaces.description: The Manung Kangjeibung (Old Manipuri: Manung Kangcheipung) (Manung Kangjeibung (Meitei for 'Inner Pologround')) is an old polo field located to the south west of the citadel inside the Kangla Fort in Imphal West district of Manipur. In ancient times, only royalties and nobilities were allowed to play the game of polo (Meitei: Sagol Kangjei) in this royal playground.

topPlaces.name: Manipur State Museum
topPlaces.description: The Manipur State Museum (Meitei: Manipur Pukei Lankei Shanglen) is an institution displaying a collection of artistic, cultural, historical and scientific artefacts and relics in Imphal, Manipur, India. It has galleries housing materials of natural history, ethnology and archeology.

topPlaces.name: Ima Market
topPlaces.description: The Ima Market (Meitei: Ima Keithel; literally, Mothers' Market), also known as the Nupi Keithel (English: Women's Market) or the Khwairamband Keithel (English: Khwairamband Market), is a women-only market in the middle of Imphal in the Indian state of Manipur. The market hosts around 5,000–6,000 women vendors who sell a variety of products, such as vegetables, fruits, textiles, toys, fish, spices and utensils.

itinerary.title: Day 1 — Statue of Meidingu Nara Singh & around

items.note: The Statue of Meidingu Nara Singh, also known as the Statue of Maharaja Narasingh (Meitei: Meidingu Narasinghgi Mitam), is a bronze sculpture located at the Kangla Sanathong, the western entrance gate to the Kangla Fort in Imphal. Meidingu Nara Singh (1844-1850 A.D.) was a Meitei monarch and the sovereign of Kangleipak (Meitei for 'Manipur kingdom').

The Kangla Sanathong (Meitei for 'The Royal Entrance to the Kangla'), also known as the Kangla Gate, is the western entrance gate to the Kangla Fort in Imphal West district of Kangleipak (Meitei for 'Manipur').

Imphal (UK: IM-fahl, US: IMP-hul; Meitei: [impʰal]) is the capital city of the Indian state of Manipur. The metropolitan centre of the city contains the ruins of Kangla Palace (officially known as Kangla Fort), the royal seat of the former Kingdom of Manipur, surrounded by a moat.

The Pakhangba Temple, also known as the Pakhangba Laishang (Meitei: ꯄꯥꯈꯪꯕ ꯂꯥꯏꯁꯪ, lit. 'Temple of God Pakhangba'), is a Meitei temple dedicated to the God Pakhangba of Sanamahism, the traditional Meitei religion, located beside the Nungseng Eekon, to the left side of the Kangla Sanathong, the western gate of the Kangla Fort in the Imphal West district of Manipur.

Manipur (, IPA: [məɳiˈpʊɾ]) is a state in northeastern India, with Imphal as its capital city. It borders the Indian states of Assam to the west, Mizoram to the south, and Nagaland to the north and shares the international border with Myanmar, specifically the Sagaing Region to the east and Chin State to the southeast.

The Manung Kangjeibung (Old Manipuri: Manung Kangcheipung) (Manung Kangjeibung (Meitei for 'Inner Pologround')) is an old polo field located to the south west of the citadel inside the Kangla Fort in Imphal West district of Manipur. In ancient times, only royalties and nobilities were allowed to play the game of polo (Meitei: Sagol Kangjei) in this royal playground.

The Manipur State Museum (Meitei: Manipur Pukei Lankei Shanglen) is an institution displaying a collection of artistic, cultural, historical and scientific artefacts and relics in Imphal, Manipur, India. It has galleries housing materials of natural history, ethnology and archeology.

The Ima Market (Meitei: Ima Keithel; literally, Mothers' Market), also known as the Nupi Keithel (English: Women's Market) or the Khwairamband Keithel (English: Khwairamband Market), is a women-only market in the middle of Imphal in the Indian state of Manipur. The market hosts around 5,000–6,000 women vendors who sell a variety of products, such as vegetables, fruits, textiles, toys, fish, spices and utensils.

faq.q: What is the best time to visit Kangla Fort?
faq.a: The best time to visit Kangla Fort is Oct – Mar. Summers are 25–42°C and winters are 7–24°C.

faq.q: How do I reach Kangla Fort?
faq.a: The nearest airport is Imphal Airport (7 km) and the nearest railway station is Dimapur Railway Station (124 km). Roads are generally good; check seasonal/monsoon conditions before high-altitude or remote stretches.

faq.q: How many days are enough for Kangla Fort?
faq.a: 3 days are usually enough to cover the top attractions of Kangla Fort at a relaxed pace.

faq.q: How much does a hotel cost in Kangla Fort?
faq.a: Stays in Kangla Fort start from ₹900 per night, with options across 4 price categories.

faq.q: What are the top places to visit in Kangla Fort?
faq.a: The top attractions are Statue of Meidingu Nara Singh, Kangla Sanathong, Imphal, Pakhangba Temple, Kangla, Manipur.
